actions.thought: In a three-handed game, holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button is a very strong starting hand. Since I am the first to act, I want to take the initiative and thin the field or potentially pick up the blinds. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a solid opening strategy to establish control of the hand.

Holding 3s 2c (32 offsuit) in the small blind facing a raise from the button, my hand is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Playing out of position with such a weak hand is extremely unprofitable. An easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The Button raised to 2.5x. While the price to call is 150 chips into a 400 chip pot, 85o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending this hand against a button open is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am on the Button with 2h 3c, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor equity and playability post-flop, folding is the only standard and profitable play.

With the button folding, I am in a blind vs blind situation against Flashlight. Q8o is a hand that ranks in the top 40-50% of hands, making it a standard raising candidate from the Small Blind to exert pressure and take down the blinds or play with initiative. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open size here.

I am in the Big Blind with K7 offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300, requiring me to call 200 to win a pot of 400. While the pot odds are tempting, K7 offsuit plays very poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the Small Blind's raising range. It is early in the tournament, so I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with KTo in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and exert pressure, taking advantage of my positional advantage.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend against a button open, especially with the blocker value of the Ace. By 3-betting to 950, I take the initiative, potentially fold out inferior hands, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position if the big blind decides to call. This sizing represents a standard 3-bet that puts pressure on the button.

Holding 7c 3c pre-flop is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind indicates significant strength from at least one, if not both, opponents. Calling or raising here out of position would be chip suicide. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I opened with KTo from the button, but faced a significant 3-bet from Flashlight in the Small Blind. While I have position, KTo is often dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AK, AQ, KQ, and mid-to-high pairs). Given it's early in the tournament and I want to avoid high-variance spots with marginal hands, I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

K7o is a weak hand even from the button position in a three-handed game. Opening with such a hand often leads to being dominated by the blinds' calling or 3-betting ranges. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to contest the pot.

Q4o is a very weak hand to play from the Small Blind out of position. Even though the action folded to me, the risk-reward ratio for a blind steal with such poor equity isn't favorable early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am on the Button with 3s 4d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even though I have position, this hand has virtually no playability or equity. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 7c. After the Button (Pro) folded, I only have the Big Blind (Flashlight) to contend with. However, 57o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and given Flashlight's stack size and previous activity, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce suited. While the button offers a significant positional advantage in a three-handed game, J2s is at the very bottom of any reasonable opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament and the fact that I have already folded or been forced to fold in several previous hands, I will maintain a disciplined approach and fold this marginal hand rather than risking chips on a weak starter.

The button has folded, leaving only the big blind to act against me. Jack-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand, but given that I am in the small blind, completing the bet to see a flop is a cost-effective way to proceed and evaluate the board texture, keeping the pot small while maintaining some positional equity.
K3o is a weak hand to isolate the Small Blind's limp, but as the Big Blind facing a limp, I can simply check to see a free flop. I'll evaluate post-flop where I have position.

The flop is 7d 4c 3d. My hand, Jh 8d, has no pair and no strong draw, possessing only a backdoor flush draw. Betting here would be an unproductive bluff into my opponent's range. Checking is the standard play to preserve my stack, evaluate the turn, and minimize risk while out of position.

I have bottom pair (pair of 3s) with a King kicker. The board is somewhat coordinated and has a flush draw present (7d 4c 3d). My opponent limped from the small blind and checked the flop. With marginal showdown value and no strong draw, betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I will often get called or raised by better ones. Checking behind keeps the pot small, allows me to realize my equity, and lets me see a free turn card.

With Jack-high and no legitimate draw on a board of 7d-4c-3d-Ts, I have very little equity to continue aggressively. Since I checked the flop and my opponent also checked behind, it is best to continue playing defensively. Betting would represent a pure bluff with no realistic chance of forcing a fold from better hands that may have already paired the Ten. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant action on the river.

With bottom pair on a board that has now brought a Ten (7d 4c 3d Ts), my hand remains marginal with limited showdown value. Flashlight has checked twice, which indicates weakness, but betting with bottom pair is unlikely to get called by worse hands and opens me up to being check-raised or floated by draws/better pairs. Checking behind again is the safest play to see a free river and potentially win at showdown.

My hand, Jack-Eight offsuit, has no pair and no legitimate draw on a board of 7d-4c-3d-Ts-5d. The flush has completed, and several straight combinations are possible. My opponent has checked down the previous streets, indicating they likely have a marginal made hand or are also checking to showdown. Betting here would be an unnecessary bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that beats my Jack-high, and I have no reason to believe a bluff would be profitable. Checking is the most prudent decision to reach showdown and minimize losses.

My opponent has checked all three streets. I only have bottom pair, which has very little value as a bet but could potentially win at showdown against missed straight draws. Betting here would only fold out hands I already beat and get called by hands that beat me (such as a 4, 5, 7, 10, or a diamond flush). The best option is to check behind and take the free showdown.

I am on the button with Ace-high (Ah2s). In a 3-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open with to steal the blinds and maintain initiative. I will open to 250 to put pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage while being prepared to fold if faced with a strong 3-bet.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, 8-2 offsuit. Facing a raise from the Button, the most mathematically sound play is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. Pro in the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more into a total pot of 400. K6o is a standard defending hand against a Button opening range given the pot odds (37.5% equity required). While not a premium hand, folding here would be too tight against a likely wide button range.

I have top pair with a King on a monotone diamond board. While I have the 6 of diamonds, my kicker is weak. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here to see how they react to this board texture. I intend to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to evaluate the turn.

The board is heavily coordinated with three diamonds (4d, 9d, Kd). Holding the Ace of Hearts (Ah) acts as a premier blocker for the nut flush, which is a powerful asset here. Since my opponent checked, a continuation bet of roughly half-pot is a standard semi-bluff to apply pressure, represent strength, and potentially take down the pot immediately, given that my opponent's calling range from the big blind is likely wide and may contain many hands that do not connect well with this specific texture.

I have top pair (Kings) on a monotone diamond flop. While the board is dangerous due to the flush possibility, I also hold the 6 of diamonds which gives me a marginal redraw. Flashlight's half-pot continuation bet is standard for a button opener on this texture. Folding top pair here would be over-folding, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands or flushes. I will call to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the turn.

I have top pair and a weak flush draw on a board with three diamonds. The turn 7h is an insignificant card. Having check-called the flop, checking the turn is the standard line to control the pot size and allow the opponent to continue bluffing or check back their marginal hands. Betting into the pre-flop and flop aggressor doesn't provide much benefit as I'm likely only called by better hands.

The board texture is wet with a flush draw present, and my opponent's call on the flop suggests they have at least a piece of the board (a King, a nine, or a diamond draw). With only Ace-high and no pair or draw, continuing to bluff here is high variance and likely ineffective against a calling range. I will check back to control the pot size and take a free river card, maintaining my minimal showdown value while avoiding a potentially expensive mistake.

On this river, I have top pair with a weak kicker (Kings, 6 kicker) and the board has three diamonds from the flop. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, bet the flop but checked back the turn after I called. This check-back on the turn often indicates a medium-strength hand or a marginal King that wants to get to showdown, or a missed draw. Since I have showdown value with top pair, betting now is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only serves to isolate me against hands that have me beat (like a better King or a flush). Checking is the best line to either see a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff from a missed straight draw.

With Ace-high and no pair, I have very little showdown value, but my opponent has shown weakness by checking the turn and river after calling the flop. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to succeed because my opponent will likely call with any pair (4s, 7s, 9s, 10s, or Kings), which are well within their calling range. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free without risking more chips, which is the most optimal line in this spot.

I have pocket Kings (Ks Kh) on the Button. This is a premium hand and an easy open-raise to build the pot and extract value from worse hands. A 2.5x raise to 250 is a standard size that encourages action while asserting my strong holding.

Holding Ten-Five suited in the Small Blind against a Button open. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1, calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding. T5s is not strong enough to 3-bet as a bluff here, and calling risks getting squeezed by the Big Blind or playing a weak range OOP. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Qh3c is a weak starting hand with little connectivity or high-card strength. Since I am in the big blind out of position against a button raise, the risk of playing a bloated pot with a dominated hand is high. It is mathematically and strategically superior to fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot.

I am on the button with 5-3 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. While the button offers a positional advantage, 5-3 offsuit does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise or a call. Folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

3d 5s is an extremely weak hand with poor connectivity and no high card value. Playing this out of position against the Big Blind will lead to difficult decisions and likely negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding 4s 2c is a bottom-tier starting hand with very little equity. Playing this hand, even in a position like the button, is not mathematically sound given the low playability of offsuit low cards. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable hand to engage in the pot.

With Ace-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds, we have a strong starting hand. Raising to 3x the Big Blind (300) is the standard play to put pressure on the Big Blind, deny equity to random cards, and build the pot when we are likely ahead.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I have position on the SB for the rest of the hand and the pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), Q4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is often dominated in this spot. Given the early stage of the tournament and the deep stacks, I prefer folding this marginal hand to wait for better spots rather than defending with a weak range.
J4o is a very weak hand to play even from the Button in a 3-handed game. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for better starting hands.

It's folded to me in the Small Blind. Q8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise against the Big Blind in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 3x the big blind to try and take the pot now or take the lead in the hand post-flop.

Holding Ace-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind against a Small Blind raise is a strong position. Ace-high has significant equity against a wide SB opening range. Since I am out of position, I prefer to keep the pot manageable by calling rather than 3-betting, allowing me to see the flop and evaluate based on the texture before committing further chips.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped the second-nut flush draw (Qh) on a monotone board. A continuation bet is standard here to maintain the initiative and potentially take the pot down if the Big Blind hasn't connected or doesn't have a heart. If I am called, I have strong equity to improve to a flush on later streets.

The flop is highly dangerous with three hearts present. My hand, Ace-Eight offsuit with no heart, has very little equity against a betting range that likely includes a made flush or a strong King. I have no draw to improve, and calling a 50% pot bet on this monotone texture without a heart blocker is essentially lighting chips on fire. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce of spades. While suited, this hand is at the bottom of a potential opening range in a 3-handed game. Given the recent history of Flashlight 3-betting my button opens and my own active play in previous hands, I will fold this marginal hand to maintain a tighter image and avoid playing a weak hand out of position if one of the blinds defends or 3-bets.

With King-Queen offsuit in the small blind after the button folds, I have a strong hand for heads-up play against the big blind. Raising is the optimal strategy to exert pressure and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind.

Flashlight raised 3x from the Small Blind. I have 98 offsuit in the Big Blind. While I have position on the raiser, 98o is too weak of a hand to defend with profitably against a 3x raise. It lacks flush potential and will often make weak pairs or straight draws that are difficult to realize. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

5d 2d is an extremely weak starting hand with very low connectivity and no high-card strength. Even in a 3-handed game where the button has a positional advantage, this hand lacks the potential to consistently realize equity. Conserving the stack by folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here.

Holding 85o in the Small Blind against the Big Blind. This is a very weak, unconnected, offsuit hand. Even though it's only 50 to call, playing 85o out of position is a losing play. The optimal decision is to fold.

On the button, first to act. A2s is a solid hand to open-raise with. It has good playability, blocks an Ace, and can make the nut flush. A standard 2.5x open to 250 applies pressure on the blinds while keeping our risk relatively low.

Q3o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a Button raise. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand does not meet the requirements for a profitable call or 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve chips.

I hold 9-5 offsuit in the big blind, which is a weak hand with poor playability. While the pot odds offer a decent price to call, playing a weak range out of position against an aggressive button opener will likely lead to difficult spots and negative expected value. Given the tournament stage and my stack depth, it is strategically sound to conserve chips for a more favorable hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit. While button ranges in three-handed play should be wide, J3o is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range. Given the weak nature of the hand and the fact that both blinds are capable players, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. While Jack-seven offsuit is a marginal hand, I want to maintain aggression to pick up the pot pre-flop rather than just calling or folding, which keeps the pressure on my opponent.

AQ offsuit is a premium holding, especially in a blind versus blind situation. Flashlight's raise from the small blind is likely to include a wide range of hands attempting to steal the blind. I will 3-bet to 800 for value, putting pressure on them while maintaining a positional advantage. This sizing builds the pot with a strong hand and punishes marginal holdings.

Jack-seven offsuit is a weak hand and cannot profitably call a 3-bet from the big blind. Investing more chips into a 3-bet pot while out of position with a marginal holding is a losing play. Folding is the most disciplined and effective choice here.

I am on the button with 9h-6c, which is a very weak starting hand that does not warrant an open-raise or further investment. The potential to be 3-bet by the blinds or to be forced to play a weak hand out of position post-flop is too high. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand given that there are plenty of hands remaining in this tournament.

With pocket sevens in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise. A standard raise size to 3bb puts pressure on the Big Blind, allows me to take the initiative going post-flop, and often wins the dead money outright.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac2c, a strong suited ace that plays very well in a blind-vs-blind battle. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. In this scenario, I have position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. Ace-Deuce suited is ahead of much of a typical Small Blind opening range and has excellent playability. I will call to see a flop and utilize my position.

On a dry, paired flop of 8-2-8, my pocket sevens are vulnerable to overcards but likely the best hand. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot serves to deny equity to overcard floats and can get value from smaller pocket pairs or stubborn Ace-highs, while keeping the pot manageable if I encounter resistance.

On this paired board of 8d-2h-8c, I have bottom pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). Pro's lead of 200 into a 600 pot is a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet that he would make with a wide range of hands, including many overcards that I currently beat. Raising would likely only fold out the hands I am beating and get called by hands that have me crushed (like an 8 or a higher pocket pair). By calling, I keep his bluffs in, control the size of the pot, and utilize my position to see how the turn develops.

The turn brings a Queen, which is an overcard to our pocket sevens and hits part of the Big Blind's flop calling range. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands like an 8, a Queen, or higher pocket pairs, while folding out weaker floats that we already beat. Checking is the best play to control the pot size, keep his weaker hands in his range, and evaluate his action.

The Queen of Hearts is an overcard that frequently connects with a Small Blind's opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ, etc.). After Pro checks, I have a decision between betting for protection/thin value or checking back for pot control. With bottom pair (Deuces) on a paired board, my hand has decent showdown value but is vulnerable. Betting likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like a Queen, an Eight, or a mid-pocket pair) and folds out hands I am already beating. By checking, I control the size of the pot and can potentially reach a cheap showdown or evaluate the river based on Pro's next action.

The river brings the Ten of Hearts, putting three hearts and three overcards to my pocket sevens on the board. I have some showdown value against missed floats or Ace-high, but I cannot bet for value since better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Betting as a bluff doesn't make much sense given the opponent's flop call and turn check. Checking to see a showdown as cheaply as possible is the most prudent play.

On the river, the board is 8d-2h-8c-Qh-Th. I have a pair of deuces with an Ace kicker. Pro has checked twice (turn and river) after continuation betting the flop. While my deuces have some showdown value against missed high cards (like AK, AJ, or KJ), the Queen and Ten on the board are likely to have hit parts of Pro's small blind opening range, or he could be holding a mid-pocket pair (33-77) that beats me. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and won't frequently fold out better hands. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value and reach a cheap conclusion to the hand is the most consistent and safe play.

QTs is a strong suited broadway hand on the Button. Raising first in gives us a chance to steal the blinds and gives us positional advantage if called. A raise to 2.5x (250) is standard to balance our ranges while putting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four suited, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened with a 2.5x raise. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting to 850 allows me to take initiative, put pressure on the Button's wide opening range, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. This strategy helps mitigate the disadvantage of playing out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c Th, which is a weak hand to defend with against a button open and a 3-bet from the small blind. The 3-bet indicates a strong range from the small blind, and being out of position against two opponents who have shown significant strength pre-flop makes calling a negative expected value move. I should fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, QTs is a borderline hand. While we have position, a 3-bet often indicates a strong range (like TT+, AJs+, AK, KQs) that dominates our hand. Given the 3-bet sizing to 850, calling here would require playing a large pot with a hand that has significant reverse implied odds. Folding is the safest and most standard play to avoid difficult post-flop spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Six suited, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My standard opening strategy from the button is to raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Ace-Six suited has good playability post-flop due to its flush and top-pair potential.

Holding 7-2 offsuit in the small blind is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. Calling a pre-flop raise out of position with this hand is a negative expected value (-EV) play. The smart move is to fold and wait for a more playable hand, especially since I still have a healthy stack and plenty of time left in the tournament.
Holding 5s 3h offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button is a clear fold. The hand is too weak to defend, and playing it out of position would not be profitable.

6d 3h is a very weak starting hand with virtually no playability or equity in a three-way pot. Investing 100 chips to call in this spot is a negative expected value (-EV) play. The most prudent strategy is to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

With King-Four offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to play out of position against the big blind, especially with deep stacks. The most standard and conservative play is to fold rather than try to steal or limp with a weak kicker.

JTo is a decent starting hand on the Button. It is strong enough to open-raise and attempt to steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) applies pressure to the blinds while keeping the risk manageable if I face a 3-bet.

I hold Ace-King of Spades, a premium hand, in the Small Blind. The Button has opened with a standard raise to 250. Given the strength of my hand in a 3-handed game and the fact that I will be out of position post-flop, I should 3-bet to build value and gain initiative. A 3-bet to 900 (3.6x the original raise) is a standard and effective sizing in this situation.

Holding Ace-Trey offsuit in the big blind, facing a button open and a strong 3-bet from the small blind, is a clear fold. The hand is too weak to contest a pot against two opponents while out of position, and the risk of being dominated by better aces or facing a continuation bet on unfavorable flops is high. Folding preserves the stack for more advantageous situations.

I opened JTo from the Button to steal the blinds, but the Small Blind 3-bet to 900. JTo is easily dominated by a standard 3-betting range (like AT+, KT+, QT+, and high pairs) and calling here would be bleeding chips. Folding is the most straightforward and profitable decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8s 5c. While position is advantageous in a three-handed game, 85o is near the bottom of the range and generally too weak to open, especially after I have shown significant aggression in recent hands (Hands 17, 18, and 20). To maintain a balanced image and avoid over-extending with marginal holdings, I will fold this hand.

Js 9h is a mediocre hand, but in a three-handed game, the pot odds (2:1) and the ability to see a flop for just 50 more chips make completing the small blind a standard, reasonable play. Raising would be overplaying this hand, and folding is too conservative given the low cost to proceed.

QTo is a decent hand and we have position against the small blind who just limped. Checking here is perfectly fine as it keeps the pot small and allows us to see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop. Raising is an option to attack the limp, but checking avoids inflating the pot out of position with a marginal offsuit broadway hand, though we do have relative position on the SB.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board of 9s 4c 4d. While I have reasonable showdown value, the board is paired, and I am out of position. Betting into the Big Blind allows them to continue with hands that might have hit the 4 or better, while checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable.

With Queen-high and no draws on a paired 9-4-4 board, I have very little equity to continue aggressively. The opponent's check indicates weakness, but a bluff might not fold out better high cards (like Ace or King high). Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card, potentially hitting a Queen or Ten to take the lead.

The Jack of Clubs on the turn improves my hand to top two pair (Jacks and Nines). Given that both players checked the flop, the likelihood of an opponent holding a four for trips is relatively low, and I am currently holding a very strong hand for a limped pot. It is time to shift from pot control to value betting. I will bet 125 chips into the 200-chip pot to extract value from any weaker pairs or straight draws that might call, while also charging for any potential river cards.

Facing a lead of 125 into a pot of 200 on the turn, I have picked up an open-ended straight draw with my Queen-Ten. However, the Jc introduces a club flush draw to the board, meaning my 8c and Kc outs could potentially be tainted if my opponent is betting a draw themselves. I do not have the direct pot odds to call, as I need around 28% equity but only have about 13-17% to hit my straight on the river. Given the lack of proper odds and the weak showdown value of Queen-high if I miss, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

I am holding 7-5 offsuit on the button. This is a very weak starting hand with low connectivity and poor equity. There is no reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding in a 3-handed game. I will fold and wait for a more profitable opportunity to play.

I'm in the Small Blind with T7o, which is a weak offsuit hand. Flashlight folded on the button. Playing T7o out of position against the Big Blind is unappealing due to its poor equity and lack of playability. The standard and most profitable decision is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.
J3s is too weak to open raise even from the Button, and limping is generally sub-optimal. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s3s. The action has folded to me. While 53s is a suited connector, it is quite weak and playing out of position against Flashlight (Big Blind) is not ideal. Since we are early in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, I will choose to fold this hand rather than risk chips on a marginal speculative hand from the small blind.

I am on the button with 5-3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and generally not worth playing, even from the button in a 3-handed game. My stack is healthy, and there's no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand when I can wait for better spots.

With the button (Flash) folding, I am now in a heads-up situation against the big blind (Pro). Holding 8d 7s, which is a marginal hand, I prefer to raise to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Raising to 250 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind.
53o is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the small blind, there is no reason to defend with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially on the button. I want to raise to build the pot, take initiative, and put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 300 is a standard size to accomplish this.
My hand is Q5 offsuit, which is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. The Button raised to 300 (3x the big blind). Folding is the only reasonable decision to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 8h. Flashlight raised from the button to 300. Pro folded the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600. The pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring about 33% equity. In a 3-handed game, 98o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button opening range. I will call to see the flop.

I have hit middle pair (nines) on a board that features two overcards (Ace and Jack). These cards are very well-represented in a Button's opening range. Being out of position, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they value this board. Donk-betting here would likely put me in a difficult spot against a raise, whereas checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a turn card cheaply or evaluate a continuation bet.

The flop is extremely disadvantageous for my pocket sevens, as the board contains an Ace, a Jack, and a Nine, all of which are overcards. Betting into this board would likely only be called by better hands (any Ace, Jack, or pocket pair higher than sevens) or raise me out of the hand. Since my opponent checked to me and I have the advantage of position, checking behind is the optimal play to control the pot size and take a free turn card, hoping to keep the pot small or realize equity if a seven appears.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce offsuit. In a three-handed game, any Ace on the button is a standard raising hand to put pressure on the blinds. I am in the small blind with a weak offsuit hand (10c, 9s). Playing out of position against a button open is strategically unfavorable, and this hand does not have enough equity to justify a call or a 3-bet. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button, I have terrible playability out of position and poor equity. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and preserve my stack.

Holding Ten-Six offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a weak hand with poor playability. Given that I have two opponents to act behind me, calling is mathematically suboptimal, and raising as a bluff with such weak cards is too risky. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous opportunity.

With A4o in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a clear open. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to pressure the big blind, hoping to take down the pot right now or play heads-up with a high card advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. This is a standard blind-vs-blind situation. A6o is definitely ahead of the range of hands Pro is likely raising from the SB. While I could 3-bet (raise) to take down the pot immediately, calling allows me to play a pot in position with a decent Ace, especially since I have a stack advantage. Given Pro's aggressive but somewhat standard play, calling is a solid way to control the pot size and utilize my position post-flop.

The flop comes with a King, which heavily favors my perceived pre-flop raising range from the small blind. I have an overcard with the Ace and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (needing a 3), giving me some backup equity if called. A continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200) is standard here to fold out underpairs, floating broadway cards, and weaker Ace-highs, allowing me to take down the pot profitably.

On this King-high flop, I have Ace-high. Pro led out with a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600. While I don't have a piece of the board, Ace-high still has decent showdown value and some backdoor potential. Pro could be betting any two cards here or a weak King. Folding is too weak against a small bet in position. I will call to see a turn card and potentially outplay them on later streets or improve to an Ace, while keeping the pot manageable.

The 7c on the turn doesn't improve my hand, and my opponent's flop calling range includes many hands that won't fold to a second barrel, such as Kx, flush draws, and straight draws. I still have a gutshot to the wheel and Ace-high, which gives me some equity and slight showdown value. Checking for pot control is the most sound decision here, allowing me to potentially realize my equity for free or re-evaluate if facing a bet.

On the turn SEVEN OF CLUBS (7c), the board is now 5h-Kc-2h-7c. The Small Blind (Pro) has checked. My Ace-high (Ah-6c) still has decent showdown value in a blind-vs-blind battle, as Pro's check often indicates a lack of a King or a desire to reach a cheap showdown with a marginal hand like a middle pair or a draw. Betting here might force out some weaker hands, but it also risks getting check-raised by a trap or a semi-bluff. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot, maintain my position, and can potentially win at showdown or evaluate the river action based on what Pro does. There is no need to turn my hand into a bluff yet when I might still have the best hand.

With Ace-high and a weak kicker on a board where all the draws missed, my hand has some showdown value but absolutely no value-betting potential. By checking, I allow my opponent to check behind with missed draws (which I beat) or marginal pairs (which I lose to, but save money against). If my opponent bets, A4o is too weak to be a reliable bluff-catcher, making this a comfortable check-fold situation.

On the river Jack of Spades (Js), the board is 5h-Kc-2h-7c-Js. The Small Blind (Pro) has checked for the second time in a row after leading pre-flop and on the flop. Pro's checks on the turn and river suggest they are likely giving up on a bluff or holding a marginal hand they want to take to showdown cheaply. By checking behind, I can win against missed draws or lower Ace-highs without risking any more chips.
With 9h 6d offsuit from the button, my hand is far too weak to consider opening. Folding is the standard and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (2-3 offsuit). The small blind has completed, so the pot is already 200. Checking is the standard, low-risk play here, allowing me to see a flop for free without committing further chips to a sub-optimal hand.

On a board of Js-4c-Jd, I have hit bottom pair with my 8s-4s. The board is paired with Jacks, which makes my pair of Fours somewhat vulnerable, but it's a decent hand in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. The flop is Js 4c Jd and I hold 2h 3d. I have no pair, no draw, and virtually no equity in this pot. The board is Js 4c Jd Ts and I hold 2h 3d. I have no pair, no draws, and no equity in the pot. My opponent (Flash) checked the turn, indicating either weakness or a trap. I have completed an 8-high flush on the river (As-Js-Ts-8s-4s). The board is Js-4c-Jd-Ts-As. I hold 2h 3d on a board of Js 4c Jd Ts As. I have absolutely no pair and no way to beat any hand that Flash might be holding. Even if Flash is bluffing, my hand has zero showdown value. I am on the button with 8d 4c, a weak hand. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, but this hand has very little post-flop playability against two opponents. 76o is a weak, unsuited connector. Even though I would have positional advantage post-flop against the Small Blind, facing a 3x raise makes the price too steep to profitably defend such a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand for 3-handed play. Since no one has acted yet and I am in the best position, I should raise to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and the table dynamic.
Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand and easily dominated. Facing a button raise from the small blind, folding is the standard and correct play to conserve chips.

I am in the big blind with Jc 8s. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, requiring me to call 200 more. While J8 offsuit is a marginal hand, the pot odds are 2.25:1. I am on the Button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in 3-handed play. I am on the button with AJo, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Standard play is to raise to gain control of the pot and build value. I have a healthy chip lead and want to put pressure on the blinds. A 3x open (300 chips) has been my consistent sizing and is appropriate here.

QhTh is a strong suited broadway hand that plays very well in position. As the first to act on the Button, this is a standard open. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position if called.

I hold 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with no equity. The small blind has 3-bet the button's raise, indicating a strong range. There is no strategic reason to invest more chips in a hand with such poor potential against two active opponents. Folding is the correct decision.

With pocket Tens on a Queen-high board with flush and straight draws, betting will likely only get called by better hands (like top pair or better) or strong draws. By checking behind, I can exercise pot control, keep my opponent's bluffing range wide for later streets, and avoid facing a difficult decision if check-raised.

After the flop went check-check, Pro's range seems capped or they are potentially slow-playing a monster. A bet of 800, which is roughly 45% of the pot, is a good size to get value while remaining disciplined if Pro raises, indicating they were slow-playing or have improved.

On the button with J2s, a very weak and speculative hand. Even though stealing the blinds is an option, the hand lacks playability if called or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to preserve the stack.

My starting hand, 8-2 offsuit, is extremely weak and provides no strategic value. I am in the Small Blind with T2o, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight folded on the Button, so I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Raising with such a weak hand out of position is unprofitable, and completing the blind with T2o is also a losing play. The optimal decision is to fold.
